content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 28,
1994
Ms. Pat Manning
City Clerk
30101 Agoura Court,
Ste.
102
Agoura Hills,
CA 91301—4335
Re:
Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No.
1—94-007
Dear Ms.
Manning:
You have requested advice concerning the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act.
1
Since your questions are
general in nature,
we are treating your letter as a request for
informal assistance.
2
QUESTIONS
1.
Are candidates who are endorsed on billboards required
to report the cost of the billboards on their campaign disclosure
statements?
2.
If the billboards were an in-kind contribution by
businesses, are the businesses obligated to report the activity
even though they had not collected $1,000 and had not filed a
Statement of Organization?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Candidates must report all contributions received and
must itemize contributions of $100 or more received from a single
source.
However, if the billboards were an independent
expenditure, the candidates endorsed would not report anything
regardless of the value of the billboards.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Sections
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Manning, Pat
File No.
1—94—007
Page
2
2.
If less than $1,000 is independently expended to endorse
candidates, or if less than $1,000 is collected in a calendar year
to support or oppose candidates or committees, the entities making
the expenditures or collecting the money have no filing
obligations.
FACTS
You are aware of three billboards endorsing three candidates
in your upcoming city election.
Your office has not received any
filings by the entities responsible for the billboards nor have
any of the candidates supported in the billboards reported these
billboards as non—monetary contributions.
ANALYSIS
The billboards are either a non—monetary contribution or an
independent expenditure.
If they were purchased “at the behest”
of any of the candidates, they are “contributions,” but if they
were done independently of the candidates, they would be
“independent expenditures.”
Regulation 18215(b)
provides that:
“Made at the behest” means a payment made under the
control or at the direction of,
in cooperation,
consultation,
coordination,
or concert with,
or at
the request or suggestion or a candidate,
controlled committee.
Section 82031 provides that:
“Independent expenditure” means an expenditure made
by any person in connection with a communication
which expressly advocates the election or defeat of
a clearly identified candidate or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly
identified measure,
or taken as a whole and in
context, unambiguously urges a particular result in
an election but which is not made to or at the
behest of the affected candidate or committee.
If the billboards were purchased “at the behest” of the
endorsed candidates, the candidates would be required to report
the fair market value of the cost to produce and place the
advertisements on the billboards as non—monetary contributions.
If the billboards had a value of $100 or more, the candidates
would be required to itemize the contribution coming from the
persons or entities making the payments.
(Section 84211.)
However, if the billboards were purchased independently of the
candidates, meaning that the payments were not “made at the
behest” of the candidates, the candidates would not have any
obligation to report them on their campaign statements.
Whether any person or entity responsible for the billboards
has to file any campaign disclosure statements depends on whether


Manning, Pat
File No.
1—94—007
Page
3
the activities of that entity or person fit within the definition
of “committee.”
Section 82013 defines “committee” as any person or
combination of persons who directly or indirectly does any of the
following:
(a)
Receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more
in a calendar year.
(b)
Makes independent expenditures totaling $1,000
or more in a calendar year; or
(C)
Makes contributions totaling $10,000 or more
in a calendar year to or at the behest of
candidates or committees.
To qualify as a “recipient” committee under Section 82013(a),
a person or group of persons must received contributions totaling
$1,000 or more in a calendar year.
In addition, if the businesses
responsible for the billboards pooled their funds or collectively
spend $1,000 or more for a single product or service, they would
have qualified as a recipient committee.
(Mark Advice Letter,
No.
1-93—139,
copy enclosed.)
To qualify as an “independent expenditur&’ committee pursuant
to Section 82013 (b),
a person or entity must make expenditures
totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year in connection with
communications which expressly advocate the election or defeat of
a clearly identified measure,
or taken as a whole and in context,
unambiguously urge a particular result in an election but which
are not made to or at the behest of the affected candidate or
committee.
To qualify as a “major donor” committee pursuant to Section
82013(c),
a person or entity must make expenditures to or at the
behest of candidates or committees totaling $10,000 or more in a
calendar year.
If you have additional questions, please contact me
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Wayne P.
Imberi
Political Reform Consultant

Statement of Organization?

the expenditures or collecting the money have no filing
obligations.
